coredata.dc:title: Flexible asymmetric supercapacitors made of 3D porous hierarchical CuCo2O4@CQDs and Fe2O3@CQDs with enhanced performance
coredata.dc:creator: Wei, Guijuan
Zhao, Xixia
Du, Kun
Huang, Yiwen
An, Changhua
Qiu, Shujun
Liu, Miao
Yao, Shuang
Wu, Yue
coredata.dc:description: Flexible asymmetric supercapacitors (FASCs) have attracted increasing interest in portable and wearable electronics. The practical application of FASCs in high energy density devices is limited by their low specific capacity, which can be effectively addressed by designing electrode materials hierarchically on the micro-nanoscale. Herein, well-defined 3D porous hierarchical CuCo2O4@carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and Fe2O3@CQDs architectures are rationally synthesized through a simple CQDs-induced hydrothermal self-assembly technique. Both of the as-prepared CuCo2O4@CQDs and Fe2O3@CQDs electrodes exhibit improved specific capacity, desirable rate capability and complementary potential range. A FASC (CuCo2O4@CQDs//Fe2O3@CQDs) on graphite paper delivers a high operation voltage of 1.55 V, an energy density of 39.5 Wh kg−1 at 1203.7 W kg−1, and long cycling lifespan. The excellent performance is ascribed to the good electronic conductivity with the assistance of CQDs and their unique 3D mesoporous structures with extraordinary specific surface area, which could provide fruitful active sites for electrochemical reactions. The newly developed FASC based on the Faradaic-type electrodes is inspiring, and would be promising for the applications in wearable electronic devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Flexible asymmetric supercapacitors
Transition metal oxides
Carbon quantum dots
3D porous hierarchical structures

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of 3D porous CuCo2O4@CQDs hollow microsphere structures
Electrochemical measurement with two-electrode asymmetric cell
Fabrication of the flexible asymmetric supercapacitor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (a) bare CuCo2O4, (b) CuCo2O4@CQDs-5, (c) CuCo2O4@CQDs and (d) CuCo2O4@CQDs-25, respectively. (e) TEM image and (f) HRTEM image of CuCo2O4@CQDs.

(a) XRD pattern of the CuCo2O4@CQDs, the inset is the schematic crystal model of CuCo2O4; XPS of the CuCo2O4@CQDs: (b) Cu 2p, (c) Co 2p, (d) O1s (e) C1s; (e) Adsorption-desorption isotherm with the corresponding BJH pore-size distribution curve (the inset) for the CuCo2O4 and CuCo2O4@CQDs nanocomposites, respectively.

Electrochemical characterizations of the CuCo2O4 and CuCo2O4@CQDs samples as electrode materials: (a) CV curves of the CuCo2O4-based electrodes at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1. (b) GCD curves of the CuCo2O4-based electrodes at a current density of 1 A g−1. (c) CV curves at various scan rates and (d) GCD curves at different current densities of the CuCo2O4@CQDs electrode. (e) The specific capacity values of the CuCo2O4-based electrodes calculated from different current densities. (f) Nyquist plots of the CuCo2O4 and CuCo2O4@CQDs samples, the inset is the equivalent electrical circuit of the CuCo2O4@CQDs electrode.
SEM images of (a) bare Fe2O3, (b) Fe2O3@CQDs-5, (c) Fe2O3 @CQDs and (d) Fe2O3@CQDs-25, respectively. (e) TEM image and (f) HRTEM image of Fe2O3@CQDs.

(a) XRD pattern of the Fe2O3@CQDs. (b) Adsorption-desorption isotherm with the corresponding BJH pore-size distribution curve (the inset) for the Fe2O3@CQDs nanocomposites. XPS spectra of the Fe2O3@CQDs: (c) Fe 2p and (d) C1s.

Electrochemical evaluation of the Fe2O3 and Fe2O3@CQDs samples as supercapacitor electrode material: (a) CV curves of the Fe2O3-based electrodes at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1. (b) GCD curves of the Fe2O3-based electrodes at a current density of 1 A g−1. (c) CV curves at various scan rates and (d) GCD curves at different current densities of the Fe2O3@CQDs electrode. (e) The specific capacitances of the Fe2O3-based electrodes calculated from different current densities. (f) Nyquist plots of the Fe2O3 and Fe2O3@CQDs electrodes.

(a) Schematic cartoon of the as-fabricated ASC with the CuCo2O4@CQDs as positive electrode and Fe2O3@CQDs as negative electrode. (b) CV curves and (c) GCD curves of the assembled two-electrode ASC obtained at different scan rates and current densities with a 1.55 V voltage window. (d) Specific capacity and the coulombic efficiency of the Fe2O3@CQDs//CuCo2O4@CQDs ASC at various current densities. (e) Schematic illustration of ion diffusion on the Fe2O3@CQDs and the CuCo2O4@CQDs surfaces. (f) Ragone plots of the as-prepared Fe2O3@CQDs//CuCo2O4@CQDs ASC, reported values for CuCo2O4-based ASCs are also involved included for comparison.

(a) CV curves of the FASC measured at different scan rates. (b) GCD curves of the as-fabricated FASC measured at different current densities. (c) Ragone plot of the FASC, the inset is the schematic cartoon of the assembled FASC adopted by the CuCo2O4@CQDs electrode and Fe2O3@CQDs electrode. (d) Specific capacity retention ratio of the FASC device after bending inward to the different angles. (e) GCD curves measured at 1 A g−1 for one FASC and two FASCs in series, the inset is the photograph of a red LED powered by two devices in series. (f) Cycling stability of the FASC over 5000 cycles at 20 A g−1, the inset is the SEM images of the CuCo2O4@CQDs and Fe2O3@CQDs after cycling.

head.ce:title: Flexible asymmetric supercapacitors made of 3D porous hierarchical CuCo2O4@CQDs and Fe2O3@CQDs with enhanced performance

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Flexible asymmetric supercapacitors (FASCs) have attracted increasing interest in portable and wearable electronics. The practical application of FASCs in high energy density devices is limited by their low specific capacity, which can be effectively addressed by designing electrode materials hierarchically on the micro-nanoscale. Herein, well-defined 3D porous hierarchical CuCo2O4@carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and Fe2O3@CQDs architectures are rationally synthesized through a simple CQDs-induced hydrothermal self-assembly technique. Both of the as-prepared CuCo2O4@CQDs and Fe2O3@CQDs electrodes exhibit improved specific capacity, desirable rate capability and complementary potential range. A FASC (CuCo2O4@CQDs//Fe2O3@CQDs) on graphite paper delivers a high operation voltage of 1.55 V, an energy density of 39.5 Wh kg−1 at 1203.7 W kg−1, and long cycling lifespan. The excellent performance is ascribed to the good electronic conductivity with the assistance of CQDs and their unique 3D mesoporous structures with extraordinary specific surface area, which could provide fruitful active sites for electrochemical reactions. The newly developed FASC based on the Faradaic-type electrodes is inspiring, and would be promising for the applications in wearable electronic devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Driven by the rapidly growing demand for electric vehicles, tremendous efforts have been made to design electric energy storage devices. Supercapacitors (SCs) are considered as one desirable reliable energy storage devices for flexible applications because of their fast recharge capability, high power output and long cycling lifespan [1–3]. However, most SCs are still suffered from poor energy density (<10 Wh kg−1), which mainly hinges on the specific capacitance and the working voltage of a cell [4–6]. To extend the operation cell voltage in aqueous electrolyte, the assembly of asymmetric supercapacitors (ASCs) or flexible ASCs (FASCs) with electronic-double-layer capacitors (EDLCs)//redox capacitors can efficiently utilize the potential gap between the two types of electrodes [7]. For example, various systems including reduced graphene oxide (rGO)//NiCo2S4, rGO//MnO2, MoO3//activated carbon (AC), AC//RuO2 and LiMn2O4//AC have been achieved [8–10]. Meanwhile, it is noted that the full device is severely limited by the lower capacity of the negative electrode, especially for the carbonaceous electrodes in the case of EDLCs//redox. Therefore, the design of redox//redox ASCs based on battery-type materials is desirable, since battery-like (Faradaic) electrodes have been widely investigated as the energy source of the capacitors. Such electrodes can achieve battery-level energy density while maintain a capacitor-level long life and good rate capability [11–13].

The binary metal oxides, such as NiCo2O4, ZnCo2O4, CoMoO4 and CuCo2O4, have been deemed as effective and scalable alternatives to conventional transition metal oxides (TMOs) due to their multiple oxidation states and synergetic effects in comparison with single ones [14,15]. Copper cobaltite (CuCo2O4) as an economic viability and nontoxic compound has been widely investigated as a battery-like electrode owing to outstanding redox activity and high theoretical capacity, deriving from the synergistic effect of Cu2+ and Co3+ ions [16,17]. These figure of merits make it be an efficient positive electrode material in ASC device. To improve specific capacitance of the carbon as the negative electrode, the exploration of alternative innovative materials with large specific capacitance, such as V2O5, VN, TiN, RuO2, MoO3-x and FeOx need be carried out [18–22]. Among these materials, iron oxides are good candidates to displace carbon-based materials due to their ideal theoretical capacitance, wide voltage window and environmental benign [23]. Therefore, the assembly of FASC (CuCo2O4//Fe2O3) is an optimal couple, in terms of the merits of efficiency and cheapness. Unfortunately, this kind of surface Faradaic reaction-dominated materials still suffer from low rate capability and poor electrochemical stability due to the poor intrinsic conductivity and extra contact resistance. An effective approach is to elaborate carbon/TMOs-based composites with three-dimensional (3D) porous hierarchically micro/nanoarchitectures, accelerating ion/electron transfer and thus alleviating structural degradation during the cycling process [24]. The introduction of carbon improves the electrical conductivity and facilitates the redox reaction kinetics [25]. However, it is still a challenge to simultaneously get positive and negative electrodes of 3D porous carbon/TMOs through a facile one-pot synthetic method.

As a new class of 0D carbon nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have shown tremendous potential in the fields of energy storage, optical, catalysis and biomedical applications [26,27]. The wonderful electronic properties of CQDs as electron donors-acceptors grant them wide-window potential for their expenditure as electrodes in energy storage devices [28,29]. Usually, three fascinating advantages are involved in TMOs/CQDs composites for the enhancement of the ASCs: (1) there will be more interspaces and pathways for both ions transfer and volume change; (2) small diameter of CQDs (less than 10 nm) with high specific surface areas enable it to carry more functional groups for surface wettability, and enlarge interface contact between electrodes with electrolytes; (3) the homogeneous distribution of CQDs in composites will provide uniform surface states to stabilize cycling performances. For example, it has been shown that RuO2 modified by CQDs presented an improved capacitance of 460 F g−1 at 50 A g−1 [30]. NiCo2O4 decorated by CQDs with various morphologies showed the outstanding electrochemical performance [31]. As far as we know, few works have been done by using CQDs as a structure-directing agent to assemble CuCo2O4 and Fe2O3-based micro/nanostructures as efficient electrodes in high-performance FASCs.

Herein, we have designed a universal CQDs-assisted hydrothermal method to manufacture 3D porous CuCo2O4@CQDs and Fe2O3@CQDs composites assembling by low dimensional nanoblocks. In the reaction system, the “electrostatic forces” between CQDs and metal cations drive the formation of porous hierarchical nanostructures. Electrochemical evaluations demonstrate that CuCo2O4@CQDs and Fe2O3@CQDs electrodes deliver desirable specific capacities and remarkable rate performance. Furthermore, the FASC (CuCo2O4@CQDs//Fe2O3@CQDs) not only displays high energy density of 39.5 Wh kg−1 at 1203.7 W kg−1, but also possesses outstanding cycling stability (e.g., 86.4% of the initial capacity remained after 5000 cycles). When the device was bended 120°, the performance has no change, showing a high flexibility. Practical applicability is also demonstrated through powering a light-emitting diode (LED) with the as-prepared device. The present work provides a robust strategy in the design of new-type cells for flexible electronics.
NaOH, ethanol, glycerol, Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, FeSO4·7H2O and urea were purchased as analytical reagents from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (China).

CQDs were prepared from an electrochemically anodic oxidation of alcohol by modifying reported method [32]. A solution mixture containing 1.0 g of NaOH dissolved in 140 mL of ethanol and 10 mL of water was used as electrolyte. Two pieces of Pt sheets (1.5 × 1.5 cm2) were employed as anode and counter electrode, respectively. Static potentials of 20 V (current density ∼150 mA cm−2) were applied to the anodic oxidation using a DC power. After electrolysis 24 h, dark brown CQDs solution was obtained. Then 150 mL of ethanol was added to salt out NaOH. Subsequently the supernatant ethanol was evaporated. The resulted reddish-brown solid was dissolved in 100 mL of deionized water. The obtained suspension was dialyzed 24 h to remove residual NaOH, and was further concentrated. The concentration of the as-obtained CQDs is 1 mg/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of 3D porous CuCo2O4@CQDs hollow microsphere structures
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical procedure, 0.0723 g of Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, 0.1746 g of Co(NO3)2·6H2O and 0.24 g of urea were successively dissolved in 15 mL of CQDs solution (1 mg/mL) and 15 mL deionized water (DI). After stirring for a while, the solution was transferred to a 40 mL autoclave and kept at 150 °C for 6 h. The precipitate was harvested after being washed. Then the as-obtained CuCo2O4@CQDs was annealed for 3 h in air at 300 °C with an increasing rate of 1 °C min−1, and labeled as CuCo2O4@CQDs. For comparison, the samples with different amounts of CQDs, such as 0 mL, 5 mL and 25 mL were also prepared, labeled as CuCo2O4, CuCo2O4@CQDs-5 and CuCo2O4@CQDs-25, respectively.

In a typical experiment, 15 mL of CQD solution (1 mg/mL), 5 mL of glycerol, and 10 mL DI water were mixed to form a homogeneous solution, then 0.111 g of FeSO4·7H2O was added. After they were dissolved, a translucent solution was obtained. The mixture was transferred to a 40 mL autoclave, followed by heating at 120 °C for 24 h in an electric oven. The obtained sample was thermally treated in air at 300 °C for 3 h, and assigned as Fe2O3@CQDs. For comparison, the samples with different amounts of CQDs, such as 0 mL, 5 mL and 25 mL were also prepared, labeled as Fe2O3, Fe2O3@CQDs-5 and Fe2O3@CQDs-25, respectively.

An X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD, Siemens D5005) was done to characterize the structures of hybrid materials. The shapes and microstructures were characterized on a field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Hitachi S-480) and a high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL JEM-2100UHR), respectively. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area was measured with a Micromeritics ASAP 2020 nitrogen adsorption apparatus by N2 physisorption. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) analyses were measured on an ESCALAB 250Xi system (Thermo Scientific) with Al K radiation. Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) was employed to evaluate the weight loss of the samples in a flow of air with a heating rate of 5 °C min−1 on a thermal analyzer (Perkin-Elmer PYRIS1).

Electroactive material (CuCo2O4@CQDs or Fe2O3@CQDs), carbon black and polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) with ethanol in a mass ratio of 80:15:5 were mixed to prepare the slurry and dried at 60 °C overnight. 2 mg of the mixture was coated onto a nickel foam (in an area of 1 cm2) to obtain a final working electrode. The active mass loading is 1.6 mg. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) measurements were performed on an electrochemical workstation (CHI 760E, Chenhua, China) in 2 M KOH solution. An Hg/HgO and Pt plate (15 mm × 15 mm) were utilized as the reference and counter electrode, respectively. The electrochemical impedance measurements (EIS) were carried out at 5 mV ac oscillation amplitude from 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz. Fo the CuCo2O4 based electrodes, since the electrochemical signature (e.g., cyclic voltammetry and charge/discharge curve) is analogous to that of a “battery” material, so the specific capacity (C) can be calculated by the following equations:
(1)
C m
=
I Δ t
m
where C
m (C g−1) is the specific capacity, I (A) represents the discharge current, Δt (s) represents the discharge time, and m (g) designates the mass of active materials. Considering the non-linear relationship between potential and time at a constant current, specific capacity is more reasonable for evaluating the charge storage performance instead of specific capacity.

For Fe2O3 based electrodes, the CV and GCD curves show the pseudocapacitance characteristics derived from a reversible Fe3+/Fe2+ couple. The specific capacitances (C) can be calculated by the following equations:
(2)
C =
I Δ t
m Δ V
where I is the constant discharge current (A),
Δ t
is the discharge time (s), ΔV is the potential window (V), m is the mass of the active materials (g), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical measurement with two-electrode asymmetric cell
ce:section.ce:para:
The positive and negative electrodes were fabricated by a similar method with the three-electrode cell. The positive electrode was pressed and combined with negative electrode with a 2 M KOH electrolyte to assemble the cell. The prepared ASC was carried out to evaluate the energy density (E) and power density (P). They can be estimated from the following equations:
(3)
C =
I Δ t
m
(4)
E =
I
∫
t
(
U
m a x
)
t
(
U
m i n
)
U
( t )
d t
m
(5)
P = E / t

Where I is the constant discharge current (A), Δt is the discharge time (s), V is the initial (Umin) and final (Umax) potential value, m is the active material mass (g) of the two electrode, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the flexible asymmetric supercapacitor
ce:section.ce:para: The FASC was constructed with the Fe2O3@CQDs as negative electrode and CuCo2O4@CQDs as positive electrode by using PVA/KOH membrane as electrolyte; and the flexible graphite paper was used as current collector. The CuCo2O4@CQDs and Fe2O3@CQDs electrodes were fabricated in the same way as individual electrode fabrication mentioned above. The gel electrolyte was prepared as follows: PVA (3.0 g) was dissolved into 30 mL DI water and stirred at 90 °C for 2 h. After the gel cooled down, 10 mL KOH (3.0 g) aqueous solution was added. Then the as-prepared CuCo2O4@CQDs@graphite paper and Fe2O3@CQDs@graphite paper electrodes were soaked into the PVA-KOH gel electrolyte for 5 min to allow the electrolyte to diffuse into the nanopores of active materials. This coating process was repeated three times. Then, the two electrodes and cellulose separator were pressed against each other, the device was assembled in the following order: CuCo2O4@CQDs/graphite paper positive electrode/PV-KOH/Fe2O3@CQDs/graphite paper negative electrode. Finally the device dried in air until the gel electrolyte was solidified and the deionized water evaporated.

FE-SEM was used to investigate the morphologies of the as-synthesized 3D porous hierarchical structures. As shown in Fig. 1 a, without participation of the CQDs, the obtained bare CuCo2O4 displays irregular polyhedrons with wide size distribution. When 5 mL of CQDs solution was added, irregular microflakes with different sizes and some echinus structures emerged (Fig. 1b). Further increasing of CQDs to 15 mL results in the formation of hierarchical echinus-like CuCo2O4@CQDs with a diameter ∼3 μm, where nanorods cover the surface of microspheres in every direction (Fig. 1c). Nevertheless, excessive use of CQDs (e.g., 25 mL) leads to the aggregation of porous structure (Fig. 1d), implying CQDs play a key role in the morphology of the materials. The possible growth mechanism of the CuCo2O4@CQDs is proposed as follows: The strong electrostatic interaction between Mx+ ions and negative charged CQDs may drive the growth of the crystal faces and control over the shape of nanocrystals. Then the induced crystal nuclei could grow into low-dimensional nanoblocks, which are then coalesced with adjacent ones and self-assemble into the 3D porous structure because of the decrease of their surface energy [33]. Elemental mapping (Figs. S1a–1e) clearly confirms the elements of Cu, Co, O and C are evenly distributed in the 3D porous CuCo2O4@CQDs and the mass percentage of the CQDs is determined to be 9.0 wt%. TEM study reveals these microspheres are composed of nanorods and show a hollow feature, where a large void space could be easily found (Fig. 1e). HRTEM image shows a lattice fringe of 0.46 nm, matching well with the (111) plane of cubic CuCo2O4 (Fig. 1f). TEM image of CQDs in Fig. S2 displays the spherical shape with a diameter of 3 nm. The lattice spacing of 0.21 nm is for the (100) plane of CQDs (Fig. 1e, Fig. S2), indicating CQDs have been successfully incorporated into the CuCo2O4@CQDs nanocomposites. The CQDs content of the CuCo2O4@CQDs is further determined by TGA (Fig. S3). The mass loss below 150 °C is ascribed to the evaporation of the residual solvent, the loss of adsorption water and crystalline water. The major weight reduction of 9.7% between 400 and 600 °C is assigned to the combustion of carbon species with oxygen-containing functional groups.

XRD pattern exhibits all the diffraction peaks can be indexed to the cubic spinel phase of CuCo2O4 (JCPDF card no. 001-1155) (Fig. 2 a). In addition, there is no (002) plane for graphite because of low quantity and crystallinity of CQDs. Fig. 2b–e give XPS of CuCo2O4@CQDs to demonstrate its chemical composition and the oxidation states (Fig. S4). Fig. 2b displays the high-resolution Cu 2p spectrum. The peaks located at 934.1 and 953.8 eV correspond to Cu2p3/2 and Cu2p1/2, respectively. Moreover, the satellite peaks at 941.6 and 961.8 eV confirm the characteristic of Cu2+ [34]. The Co 2p spectrum as shown in Fig. 2c displays two main peaks, Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2 at binding energies of 780.0 and 795 eV, respectively, along with a spin-energy at around 15 eV, which can be attributed to Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2, suggesting the coexistence of Co3+ and Co2+ states. Fig. 2d shows the O1s spectrum at high resolution, which could be fitted into three main constituent peaks (O1, O2 and O3). The O1 at 529.4 eV is for Cu-O or Co-O bonding [35]. The O2 component located at 531.1 eV is suggested to numerous defect sites with a little oxygen coordination in the nanomaterial [36]. Then, the O3 component located at 533.0 eV is ascribed to the physical and chemical adsorption of water on the surfaces of CuCo2O4@CQDs. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 2e) signifies a number of functional groups (OH, COO), which may bind to CuCo2O4 through covalent bonding or hydrogen bonding. The specific surface area and the pore size of material for supercapacitors are crucial for improving electrochemical performance [37]. Accordingly, the BET specific surface area of the CuCo2O4@CQDs is estimated to be 80.5 m2 g−1, much larger than bare CuCo2O4 (22.3 m2 g−1) (Fig. 2f). The BJH pore size distribution of 3D porous CuCo2O4@CQDs hollow microsphere structures shows a pore size distribution centered at 25.3 nm. These results indicate that 3D porous CuCo2O4@CQDs hollow microsphere structures possess larger surface area and abundant macropores, which is beneficial for the penetration of the OH− and the mitigation of volume change during the electrochemical test process. What's more, the unique nanoarchitectures can offer abundant active sites exposure for electrochemical reactions and abundant channels for the ion transport.

Inspired by the merits of structural features, the electrochemical properties of the single CuCo2O4 and CuCo2O4@CQDs electrodes were evaluated with a three-electrode system by CV and GCD tests. As shown in Fig. 3 a, CV comparisons of the single CuCo2O4 and CuCo2O4@CQDs were performed at 5 mV s−1, which show the battery-type characteristics derived from Faradaic reactions, distincting from electric double-layer capacitance characterized by nearly rectangular CV curves due to the non-Faradic reactions. Though they display pairs of strong redox peaks, the integrated areas of the curves are different. The enclosed area of the CuCo2O4@CQDs electrode is significantly larger than the pristine CuCo2O4, implying the CuCo2O4@CQDs electrode has higher electrochemical reaction activity. Fig. 3b displays the GCD curves of the CuCo2O4 and CuCo2O4@CQDs at 1 A g−1. Both curves show quasi-symmetrical shape, suggesting the two electrodes possess a high coulombic efficiency and an outstanding reversible redox process. Typically, Fig. 3c gives CV curves of CuCo2O4@CQDs, the shape of CV plots indicates battery-type behavior arising from Faradaic reactions of the Co4+/Co3+ and Cu2+/Cu+ [35].
(6) CuCo2O4 + H2O + e− ↔ 2CoOOH + CuOH
(7) CoOOH + OH− ↔ CoO2 + H2O + e−
(8) CuOH + OH− ↔ Cu(OH)2 + e−

What's more, with the increase of scan rate, the CuCo2O4@CQDs electrodes reveal the battery-type behavior, such as the enhanced redox peak sharpness, smaller peak positions shift compared with that of the CuCo2O4@CQDs, which suggests that the hybrid electrode has lower polarization, better conductivity, and faster redox reaction kinetics. The GCD voltage profiles of CuCo2O4@CQDs electrode were also performed at various current densities. Fig. 3d shows the GCD curves of CuCo2O4@CQDs. The curves have a more obvious plateau corresponding to a more significant battery-type redox behavior revealing faradaic characteristics of the electrodes. For comparison, a serious of CV and GCD curves of the CuCo2O4 are given in supporting information (Figs. S5a and b). Fig. 3e illustrates the specific capacity values of the single CuCo2O4 and CuCo2O4@CQDs electrodes based on the GCD curves. It is clearly showing that the CuCo2O4@CQDs electrode exhibits higher specific capacity at the same current density. The specific capacity of the CuCo2O4@CQDs was decreased from 779.8 to 650.0 C g−1 as the increase of the current densities from 1 to 20 A g−1, which are much better than that of pristine CuCo2O4 (from 464.4 to 224.0 C g−1). Correspondingly, CuCo2O4@CQDs shows a high capacity retention of 83.4% at 20 A g−1, which is also much greater than the CuCo2O4 (48.2% capacity retention at 20 A g−1). Fig. 3f presents typical Nyquist plots of the CuCo2O4 and CuCo2O4@CQDs electrodes. In the high frequency region, the intercept on the real axis can reflect the resistance of the electrolyte (Rs). Rs values of CuCo2O4@CQD is about 0.26 Ω, which is lower than CuCo2O4 (0.5 Ω). In addition, the semicircular pattern in the middle frequency region is related to the resistance of charge transfer (Rct). The Rct values of CuCo2O4@CQD (1.09 Ω) is also much less than that of CuCo2O4 (6.18 Ω). It is obvious that the CuCo2O4@CQD electrode possesses smaller intrinsic resistance of the active material and the charge transfer resistance, indicating that the introduction of CQDs in CuCo2O4 could significantly improve their electronic conductivity and charge transfer. So CuCo2O4@CQDs electrode displays favorable ion diffusion and shows excellent supercapacitor performance especially at a high current density. The results of EIS are consistent with GCD and CV curves.

The 3D porous Fe2O3@CQDs hierarchical structures were fabricated through a similar method, followed by the annealing process in the air atmosphere. As can be seen in Fig. 4 a, without the presence of CQDs, the pristine Fe2O3 displays agglomerations of nanospheres. After 5 mL CQDs solution was added, some 3D porous structures emerged (Fig. 4b). As the amount of the CQDs solution was increased to 15 mL, the obtained sample shows hierarchical porous flowers assembled by ultrathin nanosheets, indicating CQDs are essential in controlling the nucleation and growth of the products (Fig. 4c). Whereas, the nanoflakes aggregate seriously after adding 25 mL CQDs solution (Fig. 4d). The corresponding EDS elemental mapping (Fig. S6) suggests that iron, oxygen and carbon are evenly distributed in the hierarchical nanocomposites and the mass percentage of the CQDs is determined to be 14.0 wt%. Fig. 4e and f present TEM images of Fe2O3@CQDs at low and high magnifications, respectively. The HRTEM image shows a lattice fringe of 0.27 nm, which is ascribed to the (104) plane of Fe2O3. The lattice spacing of 0.21 nm represents the (100) lattice plane of CQDs. Phase compositions of the as-obtained sample were determined by XRD (Fig. 5 a). The diffraction pattern is found to be hexagonal Fe2O3, which is consistent with the literature (JCPDS Card No. 24-0072). The (002) plane for graphite is also absent because of the low content of CQDs. As determined by N2 adsorption/desorption test, the surface area for Fe2O3@CQDs is estimated to be 123.4 m2 g−1 with pore sizes located at 3.6 nm (Fig. 5b). The chemical state of the Fe2O3@CQDs was also investigated by XPS. For the Fe2O3@CQDs (Fig. 5c), two main peaks are separately for Fe 2p3/2 around 710.9 eV and 724.5 eV for Fe 2p1/2, which matches with the reported value for Fe2O3 [38,39]. The C1s spectrum shown in Fig. 5d signifies plenty of functional groups (OH, COO), together with sp2 hybridized carbon in the CQDs [40,41].

The capacitive properties of the as-obtained Fe2O3 and Fe2O3@CQDs electrodes were measured by CV, GCD and EIS tests. Fig. 6 a manifests the CV profiles of Fe2O3 and Fe2O3@CQDs electrodes in a potential of −0.95-0 V at 5 mV s−1. The CV curves show a wide current potential response with numerous peak humps, indicating the capacitive characteristics of the Fe2O3 and Fe2O3@CQDs electrodes possess pseudocapacitance. The pseudocapacitance of Fe2O3 might arise from a reversible Fe3+/Fe2+ couple, while the identity of the charge cations involved in the redox reaction was yet undetermined [42]. Correspondingly, Fe2O3@CQDs electrode has better capacity than the pure Fe2O3 electrode. Similar behavior can also be derived from gravimetric capacitance at 1 A g−1 (Fig. 6b). Moreover, the CV profiles of the Fe2O3@CQDs electrode retain a similar shape as the scan rates increased (Fig. 6c), and the GCD curves also keep the same feature as the current densities increased (Fig. 6d). No obvious IR drop is found at the beginning of the discharge curve, indicating a low internal resistance. For comparison, the CV and GCD curves of the Fe2O3 are given in supporting information (Figs. S7a and b). From the GCD curves, the specific capacitance values of Fe2O3@CQDs electrode at 1, 2, 3, 5 and 10 A g−1 are 298.7, 277.3, 244.0, 235.9 and 221.8 F g−1, respectively. The specific capacitance over single Fe2O3 is 205.4, 142.0, 128.1, 115.6 and 102.1 F g−1 at 1, 2, 3, 5 and 10 A g−1, respectively (Fig. 6e). Remarkably, the Fe2O3@CQDs electrode shows better -capacitance and rate performance compared with the Fe2O3 electrode, which indicates that introducing the CQDs could largely enhance the electrochemical performance. The Nyquist plots of the Fe2O3 and Fe2O3@CQDs electrodes are shown in Fig. 6f. It is found that the Fe2O3@CQDs electrode possesses smaller diffusion and charge transfer resistance, suggesting that the Fe2O3@CQDs electrode has good electronic conductivity and fast ion diffusion behaviors.

Finally, we summarize some advantages of our 3D porous hierarchical CuCo2O4@CQDs and Fe2O3@CQDs structures particularly the effect of the CQDs, leading to the largely improved electrochemical performance. The reasons can be understood as follows: (1) The large interlayer in the hierarchical structures can act as “ion-buffering reservoirs” to increase the interface contact of the electrode/electrolyte and provide plentiful active sites for electrochemical reactions, leading to the improvement of the specific capacity. (2) The presence of CQDs could improve the conductivity of the CuCo2O4@CQDs and Fe2O3@CQDs composites [43,44], which can prevent the electrode polarization (supported by CV data) and increase the charge collection efficiency. (3) The functional groups and active sites of CQDs afford the electrode wettability and electrochemical reaction, giving rise to the high specific capacity and energy density.

To test the capacitive property of the CuCo2O4@CQDs as the positive electrode and Fe2O3@CQDs as the negative electrode in a full cell, an ASC was firstly assembled by the two electrodes. The procedure for preparing ASC device is schematically shown in Fig. 7 a. The Fe2O3@CQDs electrode was tested within −0.95-0 V (vs. Hg/HgO), while CuCo2O4@CQDs electrode was evaluated in 0–0.6 V. When Fe2O3@CQDs and CuCo2O4@CQDs were assembled a cell, the working voltage of the (−)Fe2O3@CQDs//CuCo2O4@CQDs(+) ASC could be extended to 1.55 V. Prior to assembling, the CV curves of CuCo2O4@CQDs and Fe2O3@CQDs electrodes are compared at 20 mV s−1 in a three-electrode configuration (Fig. S8). The mass ratio of the CuCo2O4@CQDs electrode to the Fe2O3@CQDs electrode is set to be 0.31 for the charge balance. Fig. 7b displays the CV curves of the as-fabricated ASC at various scan rates. The current increases with the scan rate, which is close to the three-electrode system. Fig. 7c presents the GCD curves obtained at various current densities. Our device is more like a battery-type capacitor according to the CV and GCD behavior. The specific capacities of the device can reach 288.3, 262.3, 238.5, 229.6, 216.1, 208.5 and 201.0 C g−1 at 1, 2, 3, 5, 10, 15 and 20 A g−1, respectively. And the corresponding coulombic efficiency is 89.7%, 93.6%, 96.3%, 98.8%, 99.7%, 100% and 100%, respectively, suggesting the efficient transport of electrons is good for the Faradaic redox reaction (Fig. 7d). In addition, the low Rs and Rct value calculated from the Nyquist plot (Fig. S9) of the Fe2O3@CQDs//CuCo2O4@CQDs ASC device indicate that the device has favorable electrical conductivity and easier charge transfer at the electrode/electrolyte interface (Fig. 7e). Fig. 7f gives the electrochemical performance of the ASCs. At 1 A g−1, the energy density and power density are determined to be 96.3 Wh kg−1 and 1201.9 W kg−1, respectively. As the current density increases, the energy density decreases slightly, and the power density increases. Even at 20 A g−1, the energy density still remains 67.1 Wh kg−1 with a power density of 23922.2 W kg−1. Therefore, our device exhibits a high power density without significant loss in the energy density. This value is considerably higher than those previously reported symmetric SCs including AC//AC (<10 Wh kg−1) and rGO//rGO (<4 Wh kg−1) [45,46], and also higher than ASCs fabricated with similar materials including CuCo2O4@CuCo2O4//AC (18 Wh kg−1), CuO//AC (19.7 Wh kg−1), CuCo2O4/CuO//rGO/Fe2O3 (33 Wh kg−1), CuCo2O4@Co(OH)2//AG (19.2 Wh kg−1), DS-CuCo2O4//AC (37.3 Wh kg−1), HO-CuCo2O4//AC (42.8 Wh kg−1), GW-CuCo2O4//rGO (45.2 Wh kg−1) and CuCo2O4/NiO//AC (51.8 Wh kg−1) [46–54].

To assess the feasibility as flexible energy storage devices for wearable electronics, a FASC was assembled with CuCo2O4@CQDs and Fe2O3@CQDs electrodes on the graphite paper, with PVA/KOH as electrolyte. The employment of pseudocapacitive materials as electrodes in flexible devices endows them high electrochemical capacity and high energy density. The structure of the as-assembled FASC is given in the inset of Fig. 8 c. Fig. 8a presents typical CV curves of the FASC within 0–1.55 V at various scan rates. The CV curves of the FASC do not display any distortion during the forward and backward scans, indicating the good capacitive performance. Fig. 8b gives GCD curves versus changing current densities between 0 and 1.55 V, from which the specific capacity is determined to be 118.3 C g−1 at 1 A g−1. Fig. 8c shows the as-prepared device achieves a high energy density of 39.5 Wh kg−1 at 1203.7 W kg−1. Even at 24063.8 W kg−1, it can still maintain an energy density of 21.4 Wh kg−1. For practical application of the FASC, the mechanical properties are also of significance. The flexibility test was performed by bending the flexible device at various bending angles, the decrease in capacity for the FASCs device is only 1.5% after bending 120°, indicating an excellent flexibility (Fig. 8d). Fig. 8e displays the GCD profiles of the device of single device and two devices connected in series. The connected device shows a 3.1 V potential window. The charge-discharge time for the tandem device features single cell at the same current density, suggesting that the capacitive performance of the each device is retained in the tandem device. In addition, the stability of the device is measured at 20 A g−1 for 5000 cycles. As shown in Fig. 8f, it reveals a quite stable cycle performance for the Fe2O3@CQDs//CQDs@CuCo2O4 FASC, remaining 86.4% of its initial capacity after 5000 cycles at 20 A g−1, which is higher than many reported ASCs such as CoO x //graphene (54.6% capacity retained, 6000 cycles), MnCo2O4@Ni(OH)2//AC (90% capacity retention, 2500 cycles), Ni/Co-LDHs//AC (75.8% capacity retained, 1000 cycles) and Co3O4@MnO2//graphene (82% capacity retention, 1000 cycles) [55–58]. The drop of the capacity originates from the consumption of the PVA-KOH gel electrolyte as well as the charge-discharge and nonreversible reaction in the active materials [22]. After cycling test, SEM images (the inset in Fig. 8f) indicate that the electrode material still maintains original shape, further demonstrating its good stability. To show the potential application of FASC, the as-prepared device can be used to drive practical electronic devices. The inset in Fig. 8e is the photograph of a red LED (1.8 V, 20 mA) powered by two connected devices, suggesting their excellent energy storage.

In conclusion, we have developed a simple and universal strategy for the synthesis of 3D porous hierarchical structures of CuCo2O4@CQDs and Fe2O3@CQDs, where CQDs are used as a structure-directing agent to induce self-assembly of low dimension nanostructures with large surface area and suitable mesoporous distribution. The obtained CuCo2O4@CQDs positive electrode displays a desirable capacity (779.8 C g−1 at 1 A g−1) and remarkable rate capability, resulting from unique component combination and hierarchical hollow structure. The obtained Fe2O3@CQDs negative electrode shows higher capacitance (298.7 F g−1 at 1 A g−1) and excellent rate capability in comparison with the single Fe2O3, indicating that the CQDs play a crucial role in improving the exceptional electrochemical performance. Due to the outstanding electrochemical performance and complementary potential range of the two electrodes, an ASC based on the CuCo2O4@CQDs and Fe2O3@CQDs as the positive and negative electrode was designed. The as-fabricated ASC displays high specific capacity (288.3 C g−1 at 1 A g−1), high energy density and power density. Furthermore, a FASC was also assembled by using the CuCo2O4@CQDs and Fe2O3@CQDs on a simple flexible graphite paper, exhibiting a high specific capacity, long-term stability, good flexibility, high energy density, and high power density. These impressive results present great potential in designing FASCs for promising applications in the flexible energy storage and conversion systems.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21771137), Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation (ZR2016BM12), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (15 C × 08010 A), the start-up fund from TJUT, and Training Project of Innovation Team of Colleges and Universities in Tianjin (TD13-5020).
